coredata.dc:title: Influence of sonication conditions on the efficiency of ultrasonic cleaning with flowing micrometer-sized air bubbles
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Tuziuti, Toru
coredata.dc:description: This paper describes the sizes of cleaned areas under different sonication conditions with the addition of flowing micrometer-sized air bubbles. The differences in the cleaned area of a glass plate pasted with silicon grease as a dirty material under different sonication conditions were investigated after tiny bubbles were blown on the dirty plate placed in an underwater sound field. The ultrasound was applied perpendicular to the bubble flow direction. The shape of the cleaned areas was nearly elliptical, so the lengths of the minor and major axes were measured. The length of the minor axis under sweep conditions (amplitude modulation), for which the average power was lower than that for continuous wave (CW) irradiation, was comparable to that for CW irradiation and was slightly larger than under bubble flow only. Not only the relatively high power for CW irradiation, but also the larger angular change of the bubble flow direction under sweep conditions contributed to the enlargement of the cleaned area in the direction of the minor axis. The combination of bubble flow and sonication under sweep or CW conditions produced a larger cleaned area compared with bubble flow only, although the increase was not higher than 20%. A rapid change from an air to water interface caused by the bubble flow and water jets caused by the collapse of bubbles due to violent pulsation is the main cleaning mechanism under a combination of ultrasound and bubble flow.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Bubble
Cleaning
Amplitude modulation
Radiation force

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Issue: Cleaning with bubbles Edited by David Fernandez Rivas and Bram Verhaagen

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Experimental apparatus. (b) Arrangement of the glass plate and nozzle.
Signal from function-generator for amplitude modulation of ultrasound (sweep operation).
Measured distribution of bubble radii from air-assisted atomizer.
Illustration of procedure used to paste grease on glass plates.

Captured images of bubble flow for sonication under sweep conditions (B+USswp(0/4)) in the absence of a plate. Note that (a) and (b) were obtained for amplitudes a max and a min in Fig. 2, respectively. Broken lines in each image show the angle formed between the upper side of the bubble flow and the horizontal line.
Captured images of time evolution of bubble flow for B+UScw in absence of plate.

Captured images of different shapes of grease on glass plate: (left) cleaned area on pasted glass plate after B+Uscw, along with definitions of minor and major axis lengths. (right) Grease surface on glass plate after water flow without bubbles and ultrasound.
Schematic diagram of grease thickness measurement locations.
Grease thickness measured at different locations.
Measured sound pressure amplitude as function of function-generator output voltage.
Absolute values of lengths of minor and major axes for different sonication conditions.
Lengths of minor and major axes normalized by lengths after cleaning with B (bubble flow only) under different sonication conditions.
Change in bubble-flow angle, transducer power, and normalized minor axis length after cleaning under different sonication conditions in presence of B (bubble flow).
Cleaned area for different sonication conditions.
Schematic diagram of bubble cleaning mechanisms: (a) cleaning with rapid change of interface from air to water due to bubble flow, (b) cleaning with water jet due to bubbles under ultrasound.

head.ce:title: Influence of sonication conditions on the efficiency of ultrasonic cleaning with flowing micrometer-sized air bubbles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), 2266-98 Shimoshidami, Moriyama-ku, Nagoya 463-8560, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes the sizes of cleaned areas under different sonication conditions with the addition of flowing micrometer-sized air bubbles. The differences in the cleaned area of a glass plate pasted with silicon grease as a dirty material under different sonication conditions were investigated after tiny bubbles were blown on the dirty plate placed in an underwater sound field. The ultrasound was applied perpendicular to the bubble flow direction. The shape of the cleaned areas was nearly elliptical, so the lengths of the minor and major axes were measured. The length of the minor axis under sweep conditions (amplitude modulation), for which the average power was lower than that for continuous wave (CW) irradiation, was comparable to that for CW irradiation and was slightly larger than under bubble flow only. Not only the relatively high power for CW irradiation, but also the larger angular change of the bubble flow direction under sweep conditions contributed to the enlargement of the cleaned area in the direction of the minor axis. The combination of bubble flow and sonication under sweep or CW conditions produced a larger cleaned area compared with bubble flow only, although the increase was not higher than 20%. A rapid change from an air to water interface caused by the bubble flow and water jets caused by the collapse of bubbles due to violent pulsation is the main cleaning mechanism under a combination of ultrasound and bubble flow.

ce:section.ce:para: The author and coworkers have previously reported that the addition of a flow of tiny bubbles (10–20μm in diameter) produced from a mixture of compressed air and water into a 141kHz ultrasonic field using an air-assisted atomizer can enhance sonochemical reactions, although bubble pulsation at these dimensions is transient [1]. Upon collapse, such bubbles in an ultrasonic field can potentially cause a shock wave or liquid jet, which can be used to clean a solid surface. The author and coworkers studied the yield enhancement of sonochemical reactions through the addition of particles of the appropriate size and amount [2], adjustment of the amount of dissolved gas [3,4], use of an appropriate duty cycle in pulsed operation [5], suppression of liquid surface vibrations [6,7], and the addition of tiny bubbles.

Cleaning under ultrasound with the addition of micrometer-sized bubbles has also been studied by other groups. For example, Ohno et al. observed the motion of bubbles on a surface under ultrasound irradiation at 20kHz and 600W using a high-speed camera, and reported that the bubbles formed clusters due to the action of secondary Bjerknes forces on a solid surface [8]. They determined that the movement of these clusters was responsible for the cleaning of the surface. Ohno et al. also found [8] that spot damage caused by ultrasonic cavitation of the sample surface did not occur in the presence of microbubbles, probably because microbubble clusters covered the sample surface. Kim et al. produced bubbles by ultrasound, and introduced them into different ultrasonic fields to clean the surface of a photomask [9]. Agarwal et al. employed this cleaning effect by ultrasonication after microbubbling for the removal of biofilms [10]. However, little is still known regarding the combination of bubbles in high-speed motion (bubble flow) from an air-assisted atomizer and ultrasonication under different conditions. Bubble flow can cause cleaning, even in the absence of ultrasound, by the impact of the gas–liquid interface on a solid surface when the bubbles collide violently with that surface. This paper describes the evaluation of the cleaned areas on dirty glass under different sonication conditions, including sweep operation (amplitude modulation) with the addition of flowing micrometer-sized air bubbles.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the experimental apparatus. In the experiment, a 105kHz continuous wave (CW) or amplitude-modulated sinusoidal wave signal was generated by a function generator (NF WF1946A) and amplified by a 55dB power amplifier (ENI 1140LA). This signal was used to drive a planar piezoceramic transducer (Honda Electronics) 50mm in diameter that was attached to a stainless-steel plate 80mm in diameter and 1mm thick. The electric power input to the transducer was measured using a power meter (Towa TAW-60A). Ultrasound from the driven transducer propagated through a stainless-steel plate into a 100×100×120mm3 (inner volume) glass tank filled with distilled water to a height of 70mm from the bottom of the tank.

Fig. 2 shows the signal amplitude of the function-generator output in the case of amplitude modulation. The signal is a triangular wave with a minimum and maximum amplitude of a min and a max, respectively, and a period of T. In the present experiment, the period T was set to 1s. The output of the power amplifier followed the cyclic changes in the signal from the function generator. In the present experiment, the maximum (a max) was 0.5V (peak to trough), while the minimum (a min) was 0, 0.125, 0.25, or 0.375V (peak to trough). These conditions are referred to below as USswp(0/4), USswp(1/4), USswp(2/4), and USswp(3/4). The continuous wave condition is referred to as UScw, and had a constant amplitude of 0.5V (peak to trough). Lastly, the label B indicates bubble flow conditions. Sonication and bubble flow production were performed simultaneously for 60s. A square-wave signal would have been simpler than the triangular wave, but was not used here in order to avoid sudden large increases in the voltage of the amplified signal, which could damage the transducer.

Fig. 3 shows the distribution of the radii of bubbles radius from an air-assisted atomizer, measured in the absence of ultrasound using a particle sizer (AEROTRAC SPR 7340). The peak bubble radius was 26.5μm, and the radius distribution ranged from approximately 13–175μm. The present method of producing tiny air bubbles in water was based on the swirling flow of a gas–liquid mixture that the author and coworkers have described previously [1]. Using a gas–liquid mixture, Evans et al. also created bubbles with a diameter one order of magnitude smaller than their nozzle diameter [11]. The shear field generated by the flow breaks the bubbles up. A flow of tiny air bubbles was generated by mixing compressed air [air flow rate: 0.7L/min, measured with an air flow meter (Azbil CMS)] and liquid water [liquid flow rate: 0.2L/min, measured with a water flow meter (KOFLOC RK1710)] and introduced into water in a tank. The tiny bubbles emerged from the 0.84mm nozzle of an aerator (handcrafted) into the water. Both a compressor (VACUTRONICS DP-90C) and a liquid pump (EFNIC GPE-PUA-2L) with a membrane contactor (Liqui-Cel G542) and a vacuum pump (ULVAC DTC-21) were used to control the extent of degassing from the water. The purpose of the membrane contactor was to provide a large-surface-area interface between the air and water to accelerate the removal of dissolved air from the water by the vacuum. The temperature of the water was maintained within 24.2–25.3°C. The degree of saturation (DOS) of dissolved oxygen in the water in the tank was measured with a dissolved oxygen (DO) meter (YSI ProODO) as an indicator of the amount of dissolved air. The DOS was 81.0–82.0% before and 80.6–83.7% after sonication and/or bubble flow, respectively. A lower concentration of dissolved gas should help maintain a larger distance between bubbles, preventing their coalescence into larger bubbles. Therefore, the individual bubble dynamics will be violent, and suitable for ultrasonic cleaning, because the lower concentration will result in fewer bubbles and a higher pressure amplitude.

A higher concentration of dissolved gas can cause the appearance of a larger number of bubbles. The absorption and damping of ultrasound by bubbles becomes larger as the number of bubbles increases, which results in a lower sound pressure amplitude.

One half of a 20×10×1mm3 glass plate (i.e., a 10×10mm2 area) was pasted uniformly with 10mg of silicon grease and was placed 6mm from the nozzle, as shown in Fig. 1(b), in order to evaluate changes in the area cleaned by bubble flow under different sonication conditions. The grease is normally used as a high vacuum sealant. Fig. 4 shows a sketch of the procedure used to paste the grease on the glass plate. An amount of grease was added to the edge of the upper side of a carrier plate, and a glass plate was placed in contact with the grease at the edge of the carrier plate. Then, the surface of the glass plate was brought down flat onto the top of the grease-covered area on the surface plate until both plates were parallel, so that a 10mm length of the glass plate was pressed into the grease on the carrier plate. The glass plate was then drawn laterally off of the carrier plate, sliding through the grease, resulting in a glass plate pasted with a uniform thickness of grease. Any paste that protruded from the edges of the glass plate was carefully wiped off. The glass plate was weighed before and after adding the grease. If the weight difference was not 10mg, the pasting process was repeated until the plate carried exactly 10mg of grease. The plate surface was placed at an angle of 45° to the nozzle surface, as shown in Fig. 1(b), in order to suppress excessive bubble accumulation in front of the plate, which in turn prevented microbubble clusters from accumulating near the sample surface, as described by Ohno et al. [8].

The author and coworkers previously visualized the sound field under a combination of bubble flow and sonication with luminol chemiluminescence using an experimental setup similar to that used in the present experiment [1]. The captured images revealed that the luminescent region was concentrated in the region of bubble flow, while there was little luminescence between the bubble flow and the free liquid surface. This means that most of sound energy was consumed in the bubble flow region and there was little chance of sound propagation with high amplitude between the bubble flow and the free liquid surface. Therefore, our previous conclusion that there is a low probability of reflections from the free liquid surface, appears to hold in the present experiment as well.

In sweep mode, the bubble-flow angle could be changed by the radiation force, and the flow direction did change as the power applied to the transducer was varied. Fig. 5 shows images of bubble flows captured at different times under B+USswp(0/4) conditions, but with no plate present. Fig. 5(a) was obtained at maximum power and Fig. 5(b) at minimum power. The bubble-flow angle (relative to the horizontal direction) in Fig. 5(a) is larger than that in Fig. 5(b), as indicated by broken lines in the images. At zero power, tiny bubbles spouted from the nozzle and moved in the horizontal direction. When power was added, the bubble-flow angle varied with the power level due to a balance between the ultrasonic radiation force acting on the bubbles in the vertical direction and the driving force from the nozzle in the horizontal direction. The buoyancy of the bubble swarm seemed to be enhanced because the radiation force acting on the bubbles was upward; however, the bubble swarm did not spread towards the transducer. According to Young [12], the relationship between the resonant frequency (f) and the resonant radius (R) of a bubble is expressed as follows:
(1)
fR ≈ 3.28 .

The radiation force acting on a bubble is a maximum [13] at the resonant frequency corresponding to a certain bubble radius, as expressed by Eq. (1). The frequency used in the present experiment was 105kHz, so the resonant radius of the bubble should have been 31.2μm, according to Eq. (1). This is slightly greater than the peak value of the bubble radius observed (26.5μm) in Fig. 3, but was within the radius distribution, which ranged from about 13–175μm. If a transducer is driven at a resonant frequency of 124kHz to provide a resonant bubble radius of 26.5μm, a stronger radiation force may result, and this would increase the bubble flow angle.

Fig. 6 shows the time evolution of bubble flow under B+UScw conditions in the absence of a plate. The images at 0s and 0.03s indicate that the front of the bubble flow traveled 20mm from the nozzle in 0.03s, corresponding to 0.67m/s on average. The images at 0.03s and 0.07s indicate that the front advanced by another 20mm to reach 40mm from the nozzle at an average speed of 0.50m/s. The speed of the bubble flow front decreased with increasing distance from the nozzle. The speed of the bubble flow decreases because the flow is spreading out. Because horizontal momentum is conserved, the velocity must decrease as the area increases. This figure suggests that the bubble flow reached a steady state in less than 2s, as the formation of bubbles at 2s was almost the same as that at 4s. The time required to reach the steady state was much shorter than the duration of sonication (60s).

Fig. 7 shows several captured images of grease films on glass plates. The left figure shows the cleaned area of a greased glass plate after B+Uscw operation. Because the shape of the cleaned area was roughly elliptical, it was defined as a broken ellipse surrounding an area of bare glass surface. The vertical and horizontal dotted lines in the figure indicate the minor and major axes of the ellipse, respectively. The right figure shows the greased surface of a glass plate after water flow without bubbles or ultrasound. It was found that water flow alone accomplished very little cleaning, whereas bubble flow under ultrasound reliably removed some of the grease from the glass plate. It is possible to monitor the cleaned area over time by direct observation through the back side of the glass plate, which faces away from the bubble flow. The author was able to observe changes in the cleaned area with time, although the observation was performed while occasionally stopping both sonication and the bubble flow (not shown here). The detailed examination of monitoring the increase in the cleaned area is left to a future study.

The thickness of the grease pasted on the glass plate was measured using a laser displacement sensor (OPTEX-FA CD4) in order to evaluate the uniformity of the pasted grease thickness at different positions on the glass plate. Fig. 8 shows a schematic diagram of the five locations at which the grease thickness was measured. The measurement was repeated three times at each location. Fig. 9 shows the obtained grease thickness. The dotted line in the figure shows the 0.63mm average thickness of grease over all five measurement locations. The grease thickness was uniform to within the average measurement error, which was ±0.08mm.

The dependence of the sound pressure amplitude on the output voltage of the function generator was measured using a calibrated hydrophone (Brüel & Kjær 8103) placed in the center of the pasted region of the glass plate, but without the plate and without bubble flow. The measurements were performed under degassed conditions (DOS=9.1%). Fig. 10 shows the measured pressure amplitude as a function of the function generator output voltage. From a linear fit to the measured data obtained at relatively low amplitude, the pressure amplitude at higher output voltages was estimated. Output voltages of 0.125, 0.25, and 0.375V (peak to trough) corresponded to 98.2, 198, and 298kPa of sound pressure, respectively.

Fig. 11 shows the absolute values of the minor and major axis lengths of the cleaned areas under different sonication conditions, where the cleaning of pasted plates by both sonication and bubble flow was performed for 60s. Each point represents the mean value of three measurements. Each error bar represents the range of the three measured values. Fig. 12 shows the lengths of the minor and major axes normalized by those obtained under condition B (bubble flow) only. Normalization of the data demonstrated that the change in the length of the minor axis was larger than that of the major axis when the sonication conditions were altered. Moreover, the increment in the length of the minor axis for bubble flow under the appropriate sonication in comparison with bubble flow only was slight but statistically significant as discussed later. This is probably because for the present experimental conditions, the movement of bubbles in the flow was too fast to interrupt the stable pulsation of bubbles near the glass plate. If the bubble flow speed becomes slower than that in the present experiment, the effect of sonication will appear clearly. This hypothesis regarding the bubble flow speed remains speculation at present and needs evidence.

Fig. 13 shows the observed bubble-flow angle, transducer input power, and normalized minor axis length after cleaning under different sonication conditions with bubble flow. As the minimum amplitude of the function generator output voltage (a min in Fig. 2) increased from USswp(0/4) to USswp(3/4) and UScw, the average power increased monotonically and the bubble-flow angle decreased. On the left side of Fig. 13, the bubble-flow angle decreases with the length of the minor axis, and a larger angle leads to a larger cleaned area. On the right side of the figure, the average power to the transducer increases with the length of the minor axis, and increased power results in a larger cleaned area. The findings are significant in that not only increased power but also a larger deviation of the bubble flow direction can contribute to the enlargement of the cleaned area.

The cleaned area S c is calculated using the following formula,
(2)
S
c
= π ab ,
where a and b are the radii of the minor and major axes, which are equal to half of the length of the minor and major axes, respectively. Fig. 14 shows the cleaned area for different sonication conditions. The combination of bubble flow and sonication under sweep or CW conditions produced a larger cleaned area compared with bubble flow only, although the increase in area was not higher than 20%. The increment in the cleaning area for bubble flow under the appropriate sonication in comparison with bubble flow only was slight but statistically significant.

The author made the analysis of variance on the data about the length of minor axis and the cleaning area for different sonication condition as well as bubbly flow only. The analysis was performed using a commercial software package (Excel 2003, Microsoft). The analysis of variance is a method to assay the deviation of the mean and that of the error for the samples and assess whether there is the statistical significant difference among the samples [14,15]. It was found through the analysis that p-value for the length of minor axis was 6.7% and that for the cleaning area was 8.3%. If statistical significance level of 10% is chosen, that the p =8.3% value is less than 10% should be sufficient.

Thus, a combination of bubble flow and ultrasound resulted in a larger cleaned area than cleaning with bubbles only. A schematic diagram of the proposed cleaning mechanism is shown in Fig. 15 . The cleaning can be classified into the following two categories:
(a)
Cleaning by a rapid change from an air to water interface under bubble flow.

(1) The bubble flow transports a bubble to the water-grease interface. (2) The bubble collides, and a momentary layer of air is created on the grease. (3) After the bubble leaves, the bubble flow transports water to the grease surface, where the drop of water impacts upon the grease. According to Foldyna et al. [16], compared with a continuous liquid jet, a drop or a slug of liquid jet striking a target can produce a higher impact pressure. (4) The grease is removed by this impact.
(b)
Cleaning by a water jet from bubbles under ultrasound.

(1) Bubble flow transports a bubble to the water-grease interface. (2) The bubble pulsates near the grease surface and becomes non-spherical because the water between the bubble and the grease surface is constrained and the liquid flow is restricted under compression, whereas the liquid near the bubble on the opposite side can move freely [17]. (3) The pulsation of the bubble forms a water jet that violently penetrates the bubble. (4) This water jet then impacts upon the grease surface, removing some grease.

Mechanism (a) is applicable to cleaning larger bubble-flow angles, as shown on the left side of Fig. 13. A combination of mechanisms (a) and (b) occurs during cleaning with bubbles under ultrasound at higher power, as shown on the right side of the figure, so both the interface change from air to water and water jet effects contributed to the cleaning. A lower concentration of dissolved gas allows the bubbles to be in individual violent pulsation suitable for the cleaning in the latter.

In connection with the mechanism (a), it is thought by analogy to Rayleigh collapse [18,19]; that is, when bubbles traveling quickly in a liquid collide with a solid surface, the collapse and fragmentation of the bubbles produces an impact on the solid surface.

Under mechanism (b), if a bubble traveling quickly in a liquid under ultrasound approaches a solid surface, the bubble will deliver a higher speed liquid jet than it would in the absence of ultrasound [20]. This liquid jet can also contribute to the cleaning of the solid surface.

Offin et al. [21] demonstrated that micropores became cleaner using acoustic bubbles than in the case of bulk fluid flow. Note that the oscillation in bubble size produced by ultrasound was effective for cleaning the micropores reported in that study.

The author measured the lengths of the minor and major axes of elliptical cleaned areas on glass after the application of a combination of ultrasound produced under different sonication conditions and micron-sized air bubbles from an air-assisted atomizer (bubble flow). The ultrasound was applied perpendicular to the bubble flow. The length of the minor axis under sweep conditions (amplitude modulation) for which the average power was lower than that under CW conditions was comparable to that under CW conditions and was slightly larger than that under bubble flow only. Not only the relatively high power for CW conditions but also the larger change in bubble flow direction under sweep conditions could contribute to the increase in the cleaned area in the direction of the minor axis. The combination of bubble flow and sonication under appropriate sweep or CW conditions provided larger cleaned areas compared with those achieved with bubble flow only, although the increase was not higher than 20%. The cleaning effect is likely due not only to rapid change of the interface from air to water but also to the bubble flow and water jets from the bubbles generated under ultrasound.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fundamentals of acoustic cavitation and sonochemistry
Theoretical and Experimental Sonochemistry Involving Inorganic Systems
